CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

TITLE: Reorganization of mitochondrial import pathways based on GO-CAM modelling BODY: ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/abdd22e7-10e7-4047-b102-a4d13523798b) Image From [Mitochondrial protein transport: Versatility of translocases and mechanisms - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36931257/) We have modelled each import pathway based on the start and destination, plus the maturation steps. We have modelled D. melanogaster and fission yeast, and the models are equivalent with very minor differences (eg. MIM complex in yeast is replaced by a functionally analogous system in metazoa). This is the set of terms required to describe all mitochondrial import pathways: - [x] 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport) Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria. - [x] 2. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion~ (1. In the image) alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane Narrow synonym MIM pathway (different complex in higher eukaryotes) Narrow synonym: MTCH pathway Definition: The import of alpha helical and tail-anchored proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane. Note:In yeast, the insertase is the mitochondrial import (MIM) complex, composed of Mim1 and Mim2. In higher eukaryotes, the functional counterpart MTCH performs a similar role despite lacking sequence and structural homology, reflecting convergent evolution (PMID:40782022) Model: [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003239)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003187)] - [x] 3. [GO:0030150](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0030150) protein import into mitochondrial matrix (2 in the image) Synonym exact: TOM-TIM23-PAM presequence matrix import pathway NewDefinition: The import of proteins with mitochondrial targeting sequence from the cytosol across the mitochondrial outer and inner membranes into the matrix via TOM-TIM23-PAM. Model:[[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800002828)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800007453)] move to new structure - [x] 4. GO_NEW ~TOM-TIM23-SORT protein import and lateral insertion into the inner membrane~ (2b in the image) TOM-TIM23-SORT-mediated protein insertion into the mitochondrial inner membrane Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol across the outer mitochondrial membrane and laterally inserted into the inner membrane. Typically acting on single-pass transmembrane proteins with an N-terminal targeting sequence and an internal hydrophobic stop-transfer sequence which targets them for lateral insertion. Note: Some proteins utilize this pathway but can be further processed and released from the inner membrane into the IMS. --- COMMENTS --- BY: ValWood cc @hattrill --- BY: hattrill @ValWood Looking at oxidative folding dependent IMS import. The major route is via the Mia40 system, but there is also a route where CCS1 acts in place of Mia40, acting on itself and SOD. Reference: PMID:24026195. For SOD, looks like it is also coupled with metal ion loading mediated by CCS1 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30530491/ So looks like there are at least two pathways for oxidative-folding dependent IMS transport and probably another that is independent of oxidative folding. Will continue to dig. I think refining the IMS transport target list will help - on that task just now. --- BY: hattrill A very brief summary of the yeast and human differences in the Ccs-dependent pathways: In yeast, Ccs1 is first imported via the Mia40/Erv1 disulfide relay system. Resident Ccs1 then recruits incoming Sod1 precursors by oxidative folding. A minor parallel route allows some Sod1 to enter the IMS independently of Ccs1, via Mia40 directly. In humans: CCS1 import is autocatalytic, with mature IMS-resident CCS1 acting as its own import receptor via oxidative folding. This reaction is ROS-sensitive -> mitochondrial oxidative stress directly increases IMS recruitment of both CCS1 and SOD1. Metalation and oxidative folding of Sod1: Sod1 requires copper and a zinc ion cofactors. Ccs1 directly mediates copper delivery to Sod1 and forms a transient intermolecular disulfide with Sod1 to facilitate copper transfer and disulfide bond formation simultaneously. Zinc incorporation is thought to occur independently of Ccs1, prior to the Ccs1 interaction. (this is generalised as still some debated details between human and yeast) --- BY: ValWood Could we model is as a single IMS branching pathway post TOM (this would be analogous to the amino acid biosynthesis pathways, we now only represent as a single pathway to get the same result. The GO-CAMs will provide the mechanistic details of how you arrive there. We could then add additional narrow synonyms for specific pathways. --- BY: hattrill Think that pombe is more similar to human than to S.cerevisiae for the ccs pathway. Have modelled it like this for now, with the metal ion chaperone activity part of protein maturation rather than import. [Model](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69b3372b00000249) --- BY: ValWood OK, I think yeast is a bit different. The mitochondrial version of superoxide dismutase is in the matrix not the IMS, and it isn't copper dependent (the cytosolic one is)...the mitochondrial one uses manganese as its cofactor --- BY: ValWood actually claude says Sod1 (Cu/Zn-SOD) — the cytoplasmic form Primarily found in the cytoplasm, though a small fraction can also be present in the mitochondrial intermembrane space but I don't know if this is true for fission yeast where the cytosolic form hasn't been detected in the mitochondria --- BY: hattrill my claude.ai says: In the mitochondrial IMS specifically, the same chemistry applies. CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

----- But I think I might need to look at the mechanistic differences (and whether to model them), as in the IMS, Cu(I) loading - then disuphide then Cu(I) -> Cu(II) --- BY: ValWood OK, I went down a rabbit hole today curating css1 papers. The authors didn't detect it in the IMS space but they didn't rule it out so I'll infer it. I deleted the inferences earlier but I'll add them back... --- BY: ValWood I now have this which I think is the same as yours except i) I changed the disulfide oxidase to child protein disulfide reductase and made the output of mia1 input of erv1 ii) I also have hot13 and hot15 which are small/not conserved doing something upstream of mia40, in yeast but I'm not quite sure what: --- BY: pgaudet Hi @ValWood Here's my feedback on the terms suggestions [above](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issue-4058846295) 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport) >> OK 2. GO_NEW mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion (1. In the image) >> how about 'alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane'? 3. Rename [GO:0030150](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0030150) protein import into mitochondrial matrix (2 in the image) >> rename to what ? 4. GO_NEW TOM-TIM23-SORT protein import and lateral insertion into the inner membrane (2b in the image) >> how about 'TOM-TIM23-SORT-mediated protein insertion into the **mitochondrial** inner membrane' (so we remove the 'and') 5. KEEP [GO:0045041](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0045041) protein import into mitochondrial intermembrane space >> can you confirm that this corresponds to 7 in the image? 6. GO_NEW mitochondrial beta barrel outer membrane protein insertion (5 in figure): how about 'beta barrel protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane'? 7. GO_NEW TOM-TIM22 inner membrane protein import (6 in figure) >> GO_NEW TOM-TIM22-mediated **mitochondrial** inner membrane protein insertion ? --- I also suggest you make a different ticket for the obsoletions. Thanks, Pascale --- BY: ValWood Hi @pgaudet I agree with the changes 3 rename , I'm not sure, I think this can stay as it is? 5. Yes this corresponds to 7 @hattrill do you agree? --- BY: pgaudet > 3 rename , I'm not sure, I think this can stay as it is? OK yes - I just copied what you wrote above --- BY: hattrill Yes - 3 is not a rename, new synonym and definition 5. corresponds to 3/7 on the figure - for some reason we labelled cytosolic bit 3 and IMS bit 7. --- BY: ValWood @dragon-ai-agent please add GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport) Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria.
